Question: Priam bought a jacket that was on sale for $$\text{40%}$$ off. The original price of the jacket was $$\text{$150} .$$ While the sales clerk figured the price by calculating the amount of discount and then subtracting that amount from $$\text{$150} ,$$ Priam found the price faster by calculating $$\text{60%}$$ of $$\text{$150} .$$ ⓐ Explain why Priam was correct. ⓑ Will Priam's method work for any original price?
Answer: ⓐ Priam is correct. The original price is 100%. Since the discount rate was 40%, the sale price was 60% of the original price. ⓑ Yes.
o3 Answer:
a.  “40 % off” means the store is reducing the jacket’s price by 40 % of its original cost.

   Discount = 40 % of $150  
        = 0.40 × 150  
        = $60  

   Sale price = original price – discount  
         = $150 – $60  
         = $90  

   Notice that paying the original 100 % minus the 40 % taken off leaves 60 % of the price.  
   So Priam could go straight to

   Sale price = 60 % of $150  
        = 0.60 × 150  
        = $90  

   Because 100 % – 40 % = 60 %, Priam’s one-step calculation gives the same $90, so he was correct.

b.  Yes, the shortcut works for any original price (call it P).  
   With a discount rate d (here d = 40 % = 0.40):

   Sale price = P – dP  
         = (1 – d) P  

   For a 40 % discount, 1 – d = 0.60, so the sale price is always 60 % of whatever the original price is. Therefore Priam’s method is valid for every item whose price is reduced by 40 %.

   $150 - (0.40×150) = 150 - 60 = 90.$  
But by factoring out 150 you get  
   $150 - 0.40·150 = (1 − 0.40)·150 = 0.60·150 = 90.$  
So computing 60% of \$150 gives the same \$90 in one step.  

(b) Yes.  If an item has original price P and you get a d% discount, you pay  
   $P - (d/100)·P = (1 - d/100)·P.$  
That is exactly “(100%−d%) of P,” so multiplying P by (1−d/100) always gives the sale price, for any P.
